title: Communication with language barrier — use of interpreter
candidateInstructions: You are an FY2 GP. Mrs Fatima Al-Rashid, a 45-year-old Iraqi woman, attends with her 19-year-old son Khalid who acts as an informal interpreter. She has Type 2 diabetes with poor control (HbA1c 10.2%). You need to discuss her medication, lifestyle, and arrange follow-up. Consider the limitations of using her son as interpreter.
examinerNotes: Language barrier and family interpreter station. Key issues: son may not translate accurately, patient may withhold information with son present (domestic violence screening, reproductive health), GDPR/confidentiality. Candidate must: recognise limitations of family interpreter, offer professional interpreter, check understanding of medication regime.

patient.name: Fatima Al-Rashid
patient.presentingComplaint: Attending with son for diabetes review
patient.reasonForAttendance: Son speaks: My mother is here for her diabetes check-up

hpi.character: Poor glycaemic control, polydipsia and polyuria continuing
hpi.relievingFactors: When she does take medication helps

socialHistory.occupation: Housewife, arrived in UK 3 years ago

examination.general: Overweight woman (BMI 31), sitting with son who translates
examination.neurological: Reduced vibration sense bilaterally in feet

patient.emotionalState: Embarrassed and anxious in front of son. Will answer differently if son is asked to leave. May have things she cannot discuss in front of him.
patient.communicationStyle: Responds via son. Nods and shakes head. Occasionally says 'naam' (yes) or 'la' (no) in Arabic. If given chance to speak privately would reveal she sometimes skips medication when son is not watching.
hiddenConcerns: Does not always understand the medication instructions
Son does not always translate what she says accurately
She is worried about her feet but embarrassed to mention
She would like to speak privately without son present
doNotVolunteer: Medication non-compliance — son does not know she skips doses
Desire to speak privately — only if doctor offers this opportunity
Foot symptoms — only if directly asked through proper interpretation

primaryDiagnosis: Poorly Controlled Type 2 Diabetes with peripheral neuropathy

dataGathering.criterion: Acknowledges limitations of using family interpreter
dataGathering.detailedExpectation: Explains professional interpreter should be used — offers telephone interpreter

dataGathering.criterion: Offers to see patient alone at some point
dataGathering.detailedExpectation: Offers opportunity for patient to speak privately

dataGathering.criterion: Addresses patient directly, not just son
dataGathering.detailedExpectation: Makes eye contact with patient, addresses her, not just son

clinicalManagement.detailedExpectation: Confirms patient understands each medication, timing, importance of compliance

clinicalManagement.criterion: Plans follow-up and specialist interpreter for next appointment
clinicalManagement.detailedExpectation: Books follow-up with professional interpreter arranged

interpersonalSkills.detailedExpectation: Respectful of cultural background, religious considerations

interpersonalSkills.criterion: Use of visual aids and simple language
interpersonalSkills.detailedExpectation: Offers written information, diagrams where possible

references.source: GMC: Good medical practice — patients with communication difficulties
NHS England: Patient interpreter services guidance

learningObjectives: Recognise limitations of informal interpretation by family
